title: Last Call Before March 31: The NPS Tax Hack That Can Save You ₹15,000+ Before the Financial Year Ends
content:
Content:

**Opening**

With just four days left before the financial year closes on March 31, 2026, millions of Indian salaried taxpayers are racing to squeeze every rupee of tax savings they can. But there is one powerful, government-backed savings tool that most people either overlook entirely or use only halfway — the National Pension System (NPS). If you have not yet made your NPS contribution for FY 2025-26, today could be the most financially productive day of your year.

**What You Need to Know**

- NPS offers a separate tax deduction of ₹50,000 that is not part of the ₹1.5 lakh limit under Section 80C — it sits entirely on top of it.
- Under Section 80CCD(1B), investors can claim this extra ₹50,000 deduction by contributing to their NPS Tier-I account after exhausting the ₹1.5 lakh limit — but only under the old tax regime.
- The most powerful and least-used NPS benefit is under Section 80CCD(2), which covers the employer's contribution to NPS — up to 14% of basic salary plus DA — and this benefit is available under both the old and the new tax regime.
- The ₹50,000 additional NPS deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) remains available for FY 2025-26.
- At maturity, 60% of the NPS corpus can be withdrawn tax-free, while the remaining 40% must be used to buy an annuity, which is then taxed as income.
- The March 31 deadline is hard — contributions made from April 1 onwards count for FY 2026-27, not the current year.

**Why This Matters for Your Wallet**

For a taxpayer in the 30% income tax slab, claiming the additional ₹50,000 deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) directly translates to a tax saving of ₹15,000 plus applicable surcharge and cess. That is money that stays in your pocket simply by routing ₹50,000 into a retirement account before midnight on March 31. For someone in the 20% slab, the saving is ₹10,000. Few investments offer such a direct, guaranteed, government-backed return on your money on day one.

The bigger and less-discussed opportunity is for salaried employees who can negotiate their CTC structure. Tax planners say employees can ask their HR or payroll department to route part of their cost-to-company (CTC) as employer NPS contribution, which can significantly reduce tax without increasing the employer's cost. This restructuring works under both tax regimes and can yield deductions worth lakhs over a career, with zero additional outflow for the employer.

A common mistake is treating NPS purely as a tax-saving instrument and ignoring it for the rest of the year. NPS is also one of the lowest-cost retirement investment products available in India, with fund management charges as low as 0.09% per annum. NPS allows higher deductions than PPF or EPF, which are limited to ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80C, making it a more powerful deduction tool for high earners. Starting early and contributing consistently builds a retirement corpus that compounds significantly over 20-30 years.

**The Details You Need to Know**

- Tax benefits under NPS are available in three parts: Section 80CCD(1) for employee contribution within the ₹1.5 lakh 80C limit, Section 80CCD(1B) for the extra ₹50,000 deduction, and Section 80CCD(2) for employer contributions.
- Under the old tax regime: employee contribution up to ₹1.5 lakh under 80C, an extra ₹50,000 under 80CCD(1B), and employer contribution up to 14% of salary under 80CCD(2).
- Under the new tax regime: no deduction for employee self-contribution, but employer contribution qualifies for deduction up to 14% of salary under Section 80CCD(2) for both government and private sectors.
- Only contributions to NPS Tier-I qualify for these deductions — Tier-II accounts do not offer tax benefits for private or non-government employees.
- Eligible age: Indian citizens and NRIs between 18 and 70 years of age can open an NPS account.
- Recent reforms allow subscribers to withdraw up to 80% of their corpus as a lump sum at retirement, up from the earlier 60%, while at least 20% must still be used to buy an annuity. NPS accounts can now also be used as collateral for loans of up to 25% of personal contributions.

**What Experts Say**

Capital markets banker and investment expert Aditya Garg points out that many investors are unaware that NPS provides a separate tax deduction of ₹50,000 that is entirely outside the ₹1.5 lakh Section 80C limit. According to him, NPS gives you that ₹50,000 in extra deduction not inside your 80C limit, but on top of it. This distinction is critical — most people assume that once they have exhausted their 80C limit through ELSS, PPF, or life insurance premiums, there is nothing more to do. NPS breaks that ceiling.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has notified the Income-tax Rules, 2026, to align with the new Income Tax Act, which replaces several provisions of the earlier Income-tax Act, 1961 and simplifies compliance by reducing the number of forms and disclosures. Under the new framework, the new tax regime remains the default option. This means that unless you actively opt for the old regime while filing your ITR, you will be placed in the new regime by default — and miss the 80CCD(1B) benefit entirely.

Financial planners across the country are urging their clients to evaluate regime choice carefully before the year-end. If your total deductions including 80C, 80D, and HRA exceed ₹2.5 lakh, sticking to the old tax regime is likely more beneficial. If you have no other deductions or exemptions and your employer contributes to NPS, the new regime may work better for you. A quick calculation with a CA or a free online tax calculator can clarify the right choice in minutes.

**How to Take Action Today**

If you already have an NPS Tier-I account, log in immediately at the NPS Trust portal (npstrust.org.in) or through your bank's net banking interface. Most major banks including SBI, HDFC, ICICI, and Kotak offer seamless NPS contribution through their apps and websites. Contributions made online reflect within 24-48 hours, so March 29 or 30 is your practical last window. Do not wait until March 31 — payment gateway delays on the last day are common.

If you do not yet have an NPS account, you can open one online through eNPS at enps.nsdl.com using your Aadhaar and PAN. The account opening process takes under 30 minutes and the contribution can be made in the same session. Your PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number) is generated instantly. Ensure you choose Tier-I (not Tier-II) to be eligible for the tax deduction.

For salaried employees, start a conversation with your HR or payroll team in early April about restructuring your CTC to include employer NPS contributions under Section 80CCD(2). This is a zero-cost restructuring for your employer and can yield significant tax benefits for you every single year going forward, regardless of which tax regime you choose.

**Key Takeaways**

1. The NPS ₹50,000 deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) is available only under the old tax regime and must be claimed before March 31, 2026 — a taxpayer in the 30% slab saves ₹15,000 in tax.
2. Employer NPS contributions under Section 80CCD(2) are deductible under both old and new tax regimes, up to 14% of basic salary plus DA — this is the most powerful and underused NPS benefit.
3. Only NPS Tier-I contributions qualify for tax deductions; Tier-II accounts do not offer this benefit for private sector employees.
4. The new tax regime is the default from FY 2025-26 — if you want to claim the extra ₹50,000 deduction, you must actively opt for the old regime when filing your ITR.
5. NPS now allows up to 80% corpus withdrawal as a lump sum at retirement, and accounts can be pledged as loan collateral for up to 25% of your own contributions — making it more flexible than before.
6. Salaried employees should request CTC restructuring to include employer NPS contributions from April 1, 2026, to maximise tax savings throughout FY 2026-27.
7. The deadline is absolute — contributions made on or after April 1 count for the next financial year; act before March 31 to secure this year's deduction.

**What to Watch Next**

The new Income Tax Act, 2026 comes into effect from April 1, 2026. The new law restricts adjustment of certain losses while computing total income, and loss under the head house property cannot be adjusted against income from other heads if the taxpayer opts for the concessional regime. This makes tax planning for FY 2026-27 more nuanced than ever, especially for those with home loans or investment deductions. Taxpayers should consult a CA in April to evaluate their regime choice for the new financial year.

Watch for the RBI's next Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting. Rate decisions in 2026 will influence fixed deposit returns, debt mutual fund yields, and the attractiveness of NPS equity versus hybrid allocations. India's banking system is currently benefiting from strong structural drivers, with retail credit demand remaining strong particularly in housing and personal loans, and the overall regulatory tone remaining balanced — supportive of growth but cautious about inflation. Any rate cut could make the equity-heavy NPS option more attractive relative to FDs.

Looking further ahead, PFRDA is expected to introduce more flexible NPS withdrawal norms and potentially expand the NPS Vatsalya scheme for minors. Keeping an eye on PFRDA circulars and the Union Budget announcements mid-year will help you fine-tune your retirement contribution strategy for the rest of FY 2026-27.

**The Bottom Line**

With just four days left in the financial year, contributing ₹50,000 to your NPS Tier-I account is one of the simplest, highest-return financial decisions you can make today — saving up to ₹15,600 in taxes while building your retirement corpus. Log in, contribute, and lock in your savings before March 31.

**Source**

https://www.businesstoday.in/personal-finance/tax/story/nps-and-tax-2026-this-hack-can-give-you-extra-rs50000-deduction-works-in-both-tax-regimes-520177-2026-03-11

title: OpenAI Kills Sora, Dumps Disney, and Bets Everything on a Mystery Model Called "Spud"
content:
Content:

**Opening**

In one of the most stunning strategic pivots in recent tech history, OpenAI has abruptly shut down its celebrated AI video generator Sora — just six months after launching a dedicated app and three months after signing a landmark deal with Walt Disney. The company is now redirecting every freed-up compute resource toward a secretive new language model codenamed "Spud," which CEO Sam Altman claims will "really accelerate the economy." This isn't just a product cancellation; it's a full corporate identity reset happening in real time, right before a planned IPO.

**What Happened**

* OpenAI plans to discontinue its Sora AI video generator, six months after the high-profile launch of a standalone app, as the company works to simplify its portfolio of AI products.
* OpenAI and Walt Disney are winding down their partnership, which had centered on Sora; Disney had previously agreed to license iconic characters including Mickey Mouse and Cinderella and to take a $1 billion stake in the startup entirely in stock warrants rather than cash.
* OpenAI has reportedly finished pretraining its new AI model codenamed "Spud," with CEO Sam Altman telling employees in an internal memo that the company expects a "very strong model" in "a few weeks" that can "really accelerate the economy."
* Altman announced an internal reorganization: the safety division will fall under Mark Chen in the research division, security matters will report to Greg Brockman, and Fidji Simo's department has been renamed "AGI Deployment."
* Altman has relinquished direct oversight of the company's safety and security teams so he can focus on raising capital, supply chains, and "building datacenters at unprecedented scale."
* Spud may also serve as the foundation for OpenAI's planned desktop "superapp," which would combine ChatGPT, the coding agent Codex, and the browser Atlas.

**Why This Matters**

The pivot away from Sora represents a fundamental transformation for OpenAI as an enterprise. Ahead of the IPO, OpenAI wants to demonstrate it is capable of meeting practical, concrete use cases — and thus earn consistent revenue as a service. Visual generation was always a liability magnet: lawsuits over copyright, deepfakes, and abuse imagery made it a regulatory nightmare as much as a product.

In a company projecting losses of around $14 billion for fiscal year 2026 and which will not reach profitability until 2029 or 2030 at the earliest, the question of which product to allocate scarce computing time to is not a strategic one, but a matter of survival. Sora was burning an estimated $15 million per day in operating costs with insufficient user revenue to justify it.

The bottleneck is no longer just model quality — it is money, compute, facilities, and logistics. If OpenAI is moving its CEO closer to infrastructure and financing, that is a sign the competitive edge in 2026 is increasingly tied to who can secure chips, power, and capacity fast enough to keep shipping. The Sora shutdown makes that calculus brutally clear.

**The Details You Need to Know**

* OpenAI's Stargate project, a collaboration with Oracle and SoftBank, envisions investments of $500 billion over four years in new AI infrastructure, with $100 billion immediately allocated to U.S. data centers.
* OpenAI models are evaluated along an "expert parity" scale: GPT-5 reportedly achieved 38% parity with human experts, GPT-5.2 reached 70.9%, and GPT-5.4 reached 83% — making Spud's potential benchmark the most anticipated number in AI right now.
* OpenAI has been in an active "code red" state since at least December 2025 as rivals Claude and Gemini caught up and in some cases outpaced ChatGPT.
* According to the Wall Street Journal, the Sora team will be moved to focus on longer-term projects like robotics.
* Whether Spud will be classified as GPT-6 or a more incremental GPT-5.5 remains unclear, with speculation running across both camps in the developer community.
* OpenAI also appears to be winding down ChatGPT's video functionality and taking all its animation APIs offline as part of the broader pivot.

**Industry Reaction**

OpenAI needs to close the gap with Anthropic, which has been gaining significant traction with agent-based AI systems for business customers, particularly through Claude Code. Anthropic's focus on reliable, developer-friendly tooling has earned it corporate contracts that OpenAI's broader, flashier product portfolio has struggled to match — a dynamic that almost certainly accelerated the Sora shutdown decision.

Several high-profile OpenAI commitments have sputtered throughout the year, thanks to the company's newfound frugality as well as an increasing sense of dissatisfaction from partners. Disney's exit is the most high-profile casualty, but it signals a broader cooling of enthusiasm among enterprise partners who bet big on OpenAI's creative AI vision. The abrupt collapse of a billion-dollar Disney deal — signed just three months ago — is the kind of reversal that will make future partners more cautious.

On Wall Street, the reaction is mixed. Analysts at HSBC have flagged that OpenAI may need over $207 billion in additional funding by 2030, and the decision to kill Sora just before IPO prospectus documents leak is being read simultaneously as fiscal maturity and as an alarm bell about the sustainability of the AI hype cycle.

**What This Means for You**

For **businesses and enterprises**, the Sora shutdown is a warning about building core workflows on any single AI product — even from a market leader. If OpenAI can kill a flagship product and dissolve a billion-dollar Disney deal in one memo, any enterprise dependency on niche AI features should be treated as a risk to manage. Vendor diversification in AI is no longer optional.

For **developers**, the coming weeks are crucial. Spud's release could reshape the API landscape overnight. The planned superapp merging ChatGPT, Codex, and browser Atlas suggests OpenAI wants to become a developer's entire operating environment — and Spud would be its engine. Watch the benchmark drops closely; if it significantly outperforms current models on coding and reasoning tasks, the toolchain implications are immediate.

For **investors and market watchers**, OpenAI's IPO timeline just became the most watched event in tech. OpenAI has surpassed $25 billion in annualized revenue and is reportedly taking early steps toward a public listing, potentially as soon as late 2026, while rival Anthropic is approaching $19 billion in annualized revenue. The Spud reveal will essentially be OpenAI's IPO pitch made tangible — a must-watch moment.

**Key Takeaways**

1. OpenAI has shut down Sora, its AI video generator, and dissolved its $1 billion Disney partnership just six months after launch — a dramatic reversal driven by unsustainable compute costs.
2. A new model codenamed "Spud" has completed pre-training and is expected to launch within weeks; Altman claims it will "really accelerate the economy," though no technical details have been disclosed.
3. OpenAI is undergoing a major internal restructuring, with Sam Altman stepping back from safety oversight to focus on fundraising and data center construction — signaling that infrastructure is now the real competitive battlefield.
4. The company is planning a "superapp" combining ChatGPT, Codex, and its browser Atlas, with Spud likely serving as the foundational model.
5. OpenAI has been in internal "code red" since December 2025 as Anthropic's Claude and Google's Gemini eroded its lead — Spud is the company's high-stakes response.
6. With losses projected at $14 billion in 2026 and profitability not expected until 2029–2030, OpenAI's every product decision is now filtered through IPO readiness and capital efficiency.
7. The broader AI industry lesson is clear: compute scarcity, not model intelligence, is now the defining constraint — and companies that can't control their infrastructure costs will be forced to make painful cuts.

**What's Next**

In the immediate term, all eyes are on Spud's public release, which Altman indicated could come within weeks. The developer community will dissect its benchmarks against GPT-5.4 (currently at 83% expert parity) and against Anthropic's and Google's latest offerings the moment it drops. If it lives up to Altman's "accelerate the economy" framing, it could re-energize enterprise sales and provide the narrative fuel OpenAI needs for its IPO roadshow.

Over the next few months, the restructuring of OpenAI's product organization under Fidji Simo's "AGI Deployment" division will be worth watching closely. The rebranding itself is a signal: OpenAI is no longer positioning itself as a research lab releasing interesting experiments, but as a company deploying AGI-class tools at industrial scale. Expect fewer splashy consumer features and more enterprise contracts, agent frameworks, and API products aimed squarely at replacing human workflows.

Longer term, the Stargate infrastructure buildout — $500 billion over four years — combined with a potential IPO means OpenAI is betting that the company which controls the most compute wins the AI era outright. Altman has described a future where intelligence becomes infrastructure: abundant, metered, and embedded into the systems that run companies and everyday life — sold on a meter like electricity or water. Whether that vision becomes reality, or whether the weight of $207 billion in projected funding needs collapses it first, is the defining tech story of the next 12 months.

**The Bottom Line**

OpenAI just made the most consequential bet in its history — killing its most-hyped consumer product, walking away from Disney, and going all-in on a mystery model designed to make AI economically indispensable. Watch the Spud launch date: it will tell you everything about whether OpenAI can still lead the race it started.

**Source**

https://www.tomsguide.com/ai/openai-just-killed-sora-as-company-readies-ipo-and-new-spud-model

title: The Little Girl Who Talked to Clouds
content: Once upon a time, in a small village nestled between green hills, there lived a little girl named Elara who believed the clouds could hear her. Every morning, she would sit on the old stone wall at the edge of the village and tell the clouds her secrets. She told them about the butterflies she saw, the songs the wind sang, and the dreams she had at night. The villagers smiled and thought it was a child's fancy. But Elara knew better. One afternoon, as dark storm clouds gathered, she whispered to them, "Please, be gentle today." And the storm passed over the village without a single tree falling. From that day on, the villagers listened when Elara spoke to the clouds.

title: The Little Girl Who Talked to Clouds
content: Once upon a time, in a small village nestled between green hills, there lived a little girl named Elara who believed the clouds could hear her. Every morning, she would sit on the old stone wall at the edge of the village and tell the clouds her secrets. She told them about the butterflies she saw, the songs the wind sang, and the dreams she had at night. The villagers smiled and thought it was a child's fancy. But Elara knew better. One afternoon, as dark storm clouds gathered, she whispered to them, "Please, be gentle today." And the storm passed over the village without a single tree falling. From that day on, the villagers listened when Elara spoke to the clouds.

title: Running Tesla Model 3's computer on my desk using parts from crashed cars
My astrophotography in the movie Project Hail Mary
Earthquake scientists reveal how overplowing weakens soil at experimental farm
False claims in a widely-cited paper
